MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

AMAT.headline: DRAM/HBM spend is absorbing China weakness, but total revenue slipped -2% YoY in Q1 as trailing-edge logic demand stayed soft.

shifts.trend: SemiSys mix: Foundry/logic 67%→62%; DRAM 26%→34%; NAND 7%→4% — memory substituting for logic
shifts.note: HBM technology-transition spend is the structural offset to trailing-edge deceleration

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B (-16%) FY25 → $2.1B (-7%) Q1 FY26 — rate of decline moderating
shifts.note: Taiwan +71%→+46% offset; geo mix rebalancing toward leading-edge nodes

shifts.trend: Gross margin 48.7% FY25 → 49.0% Q1 FY26 (+0.2pts); op margin compressed 4.3pts in Q1 on $253M BIS legal settlement
shifts.note: Underlying margin intact; the settlement is non-recurring

shifts.trend: SemiSys +4% FY25 → -8% Q1 FY26 YoY; AGS services +3%→+15% providing buffer
shifts.note: Trailing-edge logic softness the primary drag; AI/HBM a partial offset

shifts.trend: $253M BIS export-controls compliance settlement booked Q1 FY26 for prior China shipments; adds to $407M CAMT valuation allowance in Q4 FY25
shifts.note: Two consecutive quarters of material regulatory charges

AMAT.verdict: Thesis partially intact — HBM and advanced-packaging capex are growing share of the mix, but trailing-edge logic weakness is real and export-control enforcement cost is material. Net, AMAT is transitioning rather than growing, pending a logic-capex recovery.

AMD.headline: Data Center revenue accelerated to +57% YoY in Q1 FY26 as gross margin expanded 3pts; China export overhang persists with partial MI325 licenses carrying 25% tariff.

shifts.trend: DC revenue $16.6B FY25 (+32% YoY) → $5.8B Q1 FY26 (+57% YoY); DC now 56% of total revenue (vs 48%)
shifts.note: Instinct GPU ramp clearly re-accelerating

shifts.trend: Gross margin 50% FY25 → 53% Q1 FY26 (+3pts); DC op margin 22%→28% on favorable mix
shifts.note: Margin expansion from higher DC mix despite elevated R&D+MG&A (+39%/+41%)

shifts.trend: $440M net MI308 China export charge in FY25 → MI325 licenses granted Feb 2026 with 25% US re-import tariff; no new impairment charge Q1
shifts.note: License regime stabilized but tariff cost still embedded

shifts.trend: No customer >10% of FY25 revenue; international revenue 66%→74% Q1 FY26 — hyperscaler GPU locations diversifying
shifts.note: Positive: concentration concern from EPYC customer era not materializing for AI GPU

shifts.trend: R&D +39% YoY Q1 FY26 to $2.4B; ZT Systems manufacturing arm sold to Sanmina, design IP retained for Helios rack
shifts.note: Capex-light model maintained by offloading manufacturing to Sanmina

AMD.verdict: Story strengthening. DC acceleration confirmed in Q1 FY26 and margin is expanding. The OpenAI 6 GW warrant deal is a structural demand signal. Only risk is China tariff friction on MI325 — manageable but bears watching at next print.

AMKR.headline: Revenue accelerating (+6% FY25 → +27.5% Q1 26) and capex quintupling for Arizona advanced-packaging fab; Apple at 30% remains the single dominant concentration risk.

shifts.trend: FY25 net sales +6.2% → Q1 2026 +27.5% YoY; Computing end-market +16% FY25 → +19% Q1 on datacenter packaging demand
shifts.note: All four end markets grew Q1 — broadest acceleration in recent history

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M → Q1 2026 capex $225M (vs $80M Q1 2025); FY26 guided $2.5-3.0B — 3x step-up confirmed
shifts.note: Arizona Facility construction (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing begins H1 2028) driving the surge

shifts.trend: Gross margin 14.0% FY25 → 14.2% Q1 2026 (+0.2pts) on higher utilization; offset by material/overhead
shifts.note: Thin-margin OSAT economics persist; scale benefits expected as Arizona ramps

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8%→30% of net sales, Qualcomm 11%; top-10 customers 72% both periods
shifts.note: Concentration not worsening but not improving; Arizona is partly an Apple-anchor facility

AMKR.verdict: Thesis: AMKR is executing the right playbook — advanced packaging (2.5D/HBM/SiPh) is the growth vector and Arizona locks in US hyperscaler/chipmaker demand. Margin thin now but Arizona CHIPS Act credits ($407M direct + 35% ITC) change the FY27-28 economics materially.

AMZN.headline: AWS re-accelerated to +28% Q1 2026 and RPO surged to $364B after OpenAI added $100B to its commitment; capex $43.2B in one quarter already exceeds half of FY25.

shifts.trend: AWS revenue +20% FY25 → +28% Q1 2026; AWS op income margin 35.4% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Growth acceleration coincides with margin improvement — rare combination

shifts.trend: Capex $128.3B FY25 (vs $77.7B FY24) → $43.2B Q1 2026 alone (+78% YoY); guidance to increase further in FY26
shifts.note: Annual run-rate implied >$170B if Q1 pace holds

shifts.trend: AWS RPO $244B Dec 31 2025 → $364B Mar 31 2026; OpenAI $38B existing + $100B expansion; Anthropic $8B + $50B new commitment over Q1-Q2
shifts.note: Customer base broadening at scale — two separate >$50B commitments in one quarter

shifts.trend: AWS 35.4% op margin FY25 → 37.7% Q1 2026 despite rising infrastructure cost; Trainium chip obligations now inside OpenAI contract
shifts.note: AWS margin expanding even as TPU-equivalent build intensifies

AMZN.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating. AWS growth re-accelerated AND margins improved simultaneously — that resolves the prior debate about whether AI infrastructure is margin-dilutive for AMZN. RPO jump to $364B is the most important single number: it's durable revenue, not spot.

ANET.headline: Revenue growth re-accelerated (+35% Q1 vs +29% FY25) as AI Ethernet trials convert to deferred revenue ($5.37B→$6.20B); gross margin compressed to 61.9% from 64.1% on large-customer pricing.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue +28.6% → Q1 FY26 +35.1%; product bookings accelerating faster than recognition
shifts.note: Deferred revenue acting as a leading indicator — $826M build in a single quarter

shifts.trend: Two end customers 26%/16% FY25; Americas jumped to 84.5% of revenue Q1 FY26 (vs 79.7%) — US AI-titan concentration deepening
shifts.note: AI Ethernet fabric wins are large, lumpy, and concentrated in US hyperscalers

shifts.trend: Gross margin 64.1% FY25 → 61.9% Q1 FY26 (-2.2pts); memory and silicon supply tightening cited as incremental pressure
shifts.note: Large-order pricing discounts plus supply cost the double compression

shifts.trend: Memory/silicon supply tightening cited both FY25 10-K and Q1 10-Q; ANET procuring ahead; $413M committed inventory/capacity
shifts.note: First-to-procure posture suggests supply awareness but adds inventory risk

ANET.verdict: Story intact — the RPO build confirms demand conviction and AI Ethernet trial conversion is real. Gross margin compression is the risk to watch; if it stabilizes near 62% the thesis holds, but further erosion below 60% would signal structural pricing pressure from hyperscaler leverage.

AVGO.headline: XPU customer concentration intensified sharply: top-5 end customers ~40%→~50%, one distributor 32%→42% of revenue as custom AI accelerator orders consolidated.

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers ~40% FY25 → ~50% Q1 FY26; one distributor/hyperscaler conduit 32%→42% of total revenue
shifts.note: Single-quarter jump of 10pts in top-5 share is unusually abrupt

shifts.trend: Semi solutions +22% FY25 → +52% Q1 FY26; total revenue +24%→+29%
shifts.note: XPU ramp is driving the step-change in semi solutions growth rate

shifts.trend: Semi solutions mix 55% FY25 → 65% Q1 FY26; infra software +26%→+1% (VMware fully absorbed)
shifts.note: Company transitioning from software-driven to semi-driven margin profile

shifts.trend: Gross margin 68% FY25 → 68% Q1 FY26 (flat despite higher semi mix)
shifts.note: Mgmt warned AI rack/system leasing will compress GM — not yet visible in reported numbers

shifts.trend: FY25 capex modest; FY26 expected higher; R&D +32% to $3.0B Q1 FY26 on Two-Year Equity Awards
shifts.note: Investment phase beginning as XPU roadmap extends to next-gen nodes

AVGO.verdict: Story intact — XPU demand is real and accelerating. The key risk to monitor is customer concentration: one distributor at 42% of revenue means any hyperscaler budget shift or competitive loss hits AVGO disproportionately. Gross margin guidance for AI rack/system sales is the forward tell.

CAT.headline: Power & Energy accelerating — backlog surge and +22% Q1 revenue as data-center prime-power demand strengthens; tariff headwinds ($700M Q2 alone) are compressing margins.

shifts.trend: Power & Energy +12% FY25 → +22% Q1 FY26; Power Generation sub-segment $10.3B (+32%) FY25 → continuing strong Q1
shifts.note: Data center prime-power orders explicitly cited as the acceleration driver both quarters

shifts.trend: Backlog $51.2B at YE25 (from $30.0B YE24); Q1 FY26 backlog grew further driven by P&E orders
shifts.note: Backlog provides 12-18 month visibility; risk is tariff cost pass-through

shifts.trend: P&E margin 19.9% FY25 (flat YoY) → operating margin 17.7% Q1 FY26 (vs 18.1%) — tariffs compressing
shifts.note: $700M tariff hit expected Q2 26 alone ($2.2-2.4B FY26 total); revenue guided low double-digit growth

shifts.trend: FY26 guidance maintained: low double-digit revenue growth; $2.2-2.4B tariff costs confirmed Q1
shifts.note: Management reaffirmed outlook despite Q2 tariff headwind

CAT.verdict: Thesis intact — data-center prime-power demand is the secular driver and backlog depth gives conviction through FY26. The key risk is tariff cost absorption compressing margins; if CAT can sustain pricing power the story holds. Margin trajectory is the next critical datapoint.

CDNS.headline: Growth re-accelerated to +19% Q1 FY26 with China +36% despite BIS uncertainty; backlog steady at $8B and the BIS license whiplash (May→July 2025 rescind) has not structurally damaged the business.

shifts.note: China acceleration post-BIS rescind (July 2025) confirms demand pent up during license period

shifts.trend: RPO backlog $7.8B FY25 → $8.0B Q1 FY26 (+$200M); 53%→55% to recognize in next 12 months
shifts.note: Backlog composition stable and near-term mix improving slightly

shifts.trend: Op margin 28% FY25 → 29% Q1 FY26 (recovered from FY25 compression by $128.5M BIS/DOJ settlement charge)
shifts.note: Settlement charge was one-time; underlying margin holding at 29%

shifts.trend: BIS 'is-informed' letter May 23 → rescinded July 2, 2025; Sep 2025 50%-owned-affiliate rule adds new boundary; mgmt says 'limited' impact
shifts.note: Regulatory picture still evolving but most acute risk (full China EDA ban) was averted

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact. The BIS whiplash in May-July 2025 was a scare, not a structural impairment. China revenue is actually accelerating post-rescind. The risk is a new, broader BIS action — monitor the Sep 2025 affiliated-entity rule implementation and any new EDA-specific restrictions.

CEG.headline: Transformed from nuclear-only to 55 GW combined fleet via $22B Calpine close; Crane (TMI) restart secured $1B DOE loan; AI/data-center baseload demand explicitly framed as multi-decade tailwind.

shifts.trend: Pre-Calpine: pure nuclear fleet, ~31 GW; Post-Jan 2026 Calpine close: 55 GW (21 GW nat gas + 730 MW geothermal + nuclear + batteries)
shifts.note: Scale transformation — CEG now world's largest private power producer

shifts.trend: Adjusted op EPS $8.67 FY24 → $9.39 FY25 (+8.3%); operating revenues $23.6B→$25.5B (+8%); GAAP net income declined on lower nuclear PTC
shifts.note: Organic generation business growing; GAAP distorted by nuclear PTC timing

shifts.trend: Single hyperscaler PPAs (3E Center/Microsoft Crane) → diversified: Meta Clinton 20yr PPA Q3 FY25, Microsoft Crane, active discussions across multiple tech customers
shifts.note: Data-center PPA pipeline deepening from 1 to 3+ contracts in 18 months

shifts.trend: Crane TMI restart: $1.0B DOE loan guarantee Nov 2025 secured; 80-year license extension filings planned for entire nuclear fleet
shifts.note: Long-duration capital deployment aligns with AI hyperscaler multi-decade commitments

CEG.verdict: Thesis upgrading. Calpine close turns CEG into a diversified clean-and-dispatchable power platform just as AI data-center demand for baseload intensifies. Crane + Meta + Microsoft PPAs are locking in long-duration contracted revenue. Key risk: Calpine integration and gas-turbine regulatory exposure.

CLS.headline: Revenue growth stepped up from +42%→+53% as HPS (networking switch programs) grew 63% and the Enterprise AI-compute program that paused in FY25 came back with +101% Q1.

shifts.trend: CCS segment +42% FY25 → +76% Q1 FY26; HPS +81% FY25 → +63% Q1; total +53% Q1
shifts.note: CCS acceleration is the standout — hyperscaler switch ramps intensifying

shifts.trend: HPS 41% of revenue FY25 → 42% Q1 FY26; Enterprise AI compute (previously paused) +101% Q1 as next-gen program ramps
shifts.note: Both legs of the hyperscaler book now growing simultaneously

shifts.trend: 3 CCS customers >10% FY25 (32%, 14%, 12%); top 10 = 79%; Q1 FY26 CCS = 80% of total revenue
shifts.note: Revenue mix concentrating further toward a few hyperscaler programs

shifts.trend: Historical capex 1.5-2% of revenue → FY26 guided ~$1B (~6%); revolver upsized $750M→$1.75B to fund scale-up
shifts.note: Capex stepping to a new level — signals multi-year capacity commitment to hyperscaler demand

CLS.verdict: Story intact and re-accelerating. The HPS+Enterprise AI program restoring simultaneously is a setup for a big FY26. Concentration risk is real (one customer ~32% of CCS) but locked-in program economics reduce cancellation risk vs. a product cycle. Watch CCS margin trajectory as the next tell.

CMI.headline: Power Systems EBITDA margin expanded from 18.4% → 22.7% → 29.5% across the two reports as data-center generator demand drove pricing power; capacity is still backlogged 6-8 quarters.

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA margin 18.4% FY25 → 22.7% FY25 (Q4 implied) → 29.5% Q1 FY26; absolute EBITDA $1.69B FY25 → $577M Q1 alone
shifts.note: Three-quarter margin expansion trajectory is unambiguous pricing-power signal

shifts.trend: Power Systems +16% FY25 → +19% Q1 FY26; Power Generation $4.73B FY25 (+19%) → $1.28B Q1 (+28%)
shifts.note: Data center demand outpacing the broader power systems market

shifts.trend: 'Strong demand extending 6-8 quarters out' in FY25 10-K → same language reaffirmed in Q1 FY26 10-Q; no easing language added
shifts.note: Backlog language unchanged across two consecutive filings = persistent tightness

shifts.trend: Engine segment EBITDA -$179M Q1 FY26 on lower volumes and higher product-coverage costs; truck market weak
shifts.note: Data center is masking structural weakness in the truck-engine franchise

CMI.verdict: Power generation thesis intact and strengthening — three consecutive quarters of margin expansion with unchanged supply-tightness language is the clearest bottleneck signal in the portfolio. The key risk is the truck-engine franchise deterioration bleeding into overall financials if the data-center cycle turns.

COHR.headline: D&C growth accelerated +34%→+41% and gross margin expanded further to 38% (+243bps YoY) as 'scale across' interconnect emerged as a new demand driver alongside DCI.

shifts.trend: D&C segment +34% FQ2 → +41% FQ3 YoY; D&C revenue $1.21B→$1.36B; 9-month D&C +34%
shifts.note: Growth acceleration driven by AI datacenter plus new 'scale across' interconnect category

shifts.trend: Gross margin 37% FQ2 → 38% FQ3 (+243bps YoY in Q3); D&C segment profit +44%→+49% YoY
shifts.note: Pricing optimization and yield improvements compounding across two quarters

shifts.trend: Industrial -10% FQ2 → -16% FQ3 (divestitures of Munich + aerospace); D&C share rising to ~75% of revenue
shifts.note: Portfolio narrowing toward pure AI-optics play — strategic re-positioning confirmed

shifts.trend: New demand category added FQ3: 'scale across' interconnect (inter-DC AI fabric) joins DCI and traditional telecom
shifts.note: COHR broadening from DCI to full inter-datacenter connectivity stack

COHR.verdict: Thesis strengthening. Two consecutive quarters of margin expansion plus an expanding demand narrative (scale across added to DCI+telecom) confirm COHR is benefiting from the full optics buildout, not just one niche. Industrial divestiture cleanup is nearly done — forward story is cleaner.

CRM.headline: RPO recovered from Q3's $59.5B (down from $63.4B) to $72.4B at FY26 year-end (+14%); Informatica $9.6B acquisition closes the agentic AI data loop, though consumption-pricing upside is still unproven.

shifts.trend: Revenue +9% Q3 FY26 → +10% FY26 full-year; cRPO +16% at Jan 31 2026 outpacing total RPO (+14%) — near-term demand re-accelerating
shifts.note: cRPO outpacing RPO is the healthiest forward indicator

shifts.trend: Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack/Other +19% Q3 → primary growth vector for FY26; Informatica $0.4B contribution in FY26
shifts.note: AI consumption bucket is the only segment with >10% growth

shifts.trend: $12.7B stock repurchased FY26 (50M shares) + $1.6B dividends; OCF $15.0B (+15%)
shifts.note: Capital return intensity accelerated significantly — signals mgmt confidence in FCF durability

shifts.trend: RPO $59.5B (Q3, below peak) → $72.4B year-end (+22%); recovery confirms consumption pricing is scaling
shifts.note: The RPO dip in Q3 was a transient trough, not a demand signal

CRM.verdict: Thesis broadly intact but with a caveat: 10% top-line growth is not a software-AI compounder story. The Agentforce/consumption model upside is real but unquantified in the filings. The $72.4B RPO and $12.7B buyback suggest management has conviction; the market needs to see consumption acceleration at next print.

CRWV.headline: RPO surged $60.7B→$98.8B in one quarter; Microsoft concentration fell 67%→45% as Meta emerged at 20%; but net losses widening ($1.2B→$740M per quarter) and debt load grew to >$28B post-quarter.

shifts.trend: Revenue $5.1B FY25 (+168%) → $2.08B Q1 2026 (+112% YoY); annualized run-rate ~$8B+
shifts.note: Absolute growth enormous but YoY rate declining as base grows — still triple-digit

shifts.trend: Microsoft 67% FY25 → 45% Q1 2026; Meta emerges at 20%; top-2 still ~65% but diversifying
shifts.note: Fastest diversification in the hypergrowth cohort — OpenAI $11.9B and Meta $14.2B commitments visible

shifts.trend: Total debt $21.6B FY25 → $28.5B+ post-Q1 (April 2026: $4.0B convert + $2.8B senior notes + $1.5B revolver repaid + $1.0B equity)
shifts.note: Each quarter adds multi-billions of gross debt — interest expense rising rapidly

shifts.trend: 850 MW active power FY25 → 3.1 GW contracted total; deploying GB200/GB300 NVL72 first-to-market
shifts.note: Compute infrastructure scaling but debt financing each MW of capacity

shifts.trend: RPO $15.1B Dec 2024 → $60.7B Dec 2025 → $98.8B Mar 2026 — almost 7x in 15 months
shifts.note: RPO trajectory is the credibility anchor; 36% to be recognized by Mar 2028

CRWV.verdict: Thesis depends entirely on whether contracted RPO converts to cash before the debt stack becomes unserviceable. RPO growth is extraordinary. But net loss of $740M per quarter plus rising interest on $28B+ debt means this is a race between revenue recognition and financial stress. Not a risk-off name.

CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated +15%→+21% YoY across the two quarters driven by AI Infrastructure and hyperscaler Silicon One orders; Security segment declining as Splunk migrates to cloud.

shifts.trend: Total revenue +8%→+10% YoY; Networking product +15%→+21% ($+1.0B→+$1.4B); 1H FY26 +18%
shifts.note: Hyperscaler AI Infrastructure driving the acceleration in both quarters

shifts.trend: Security -4% Q2 on Splunk cloud migration, on-prem decline; expected to continue 2H FY26
shifts.note: Splunk headwind well-flagged but not resolved — drag on blended growth rate

shifts.trend: Silicon One purchase commitments +10% vs FYE in Q1 → ongoing build in Q2; inventory +7% both quarters
shifts.note: CSCO is pre-committing capacity for hyperscaler demand — supply confidence signal

shifts.trend: Product GM -0.6pts Q1 on mix/pricing → +0.2pts Q2 on productivity; swings modest, underlying stable
shifts.note: Mix shift to AI Infrastructure hardware (lower-margin) offset by manufacturing productivity

CSCO.verdict: Networking thesis intact. CSCO is executing as the Ethernet-switching incumbent for AI scale-out. The risk is Security's Splunk drag and whether AI Infrastructure displaces traditional Cisco or Cisco captures it. Two-quarter acceleration in Networking suggests capture is happening.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas organic orders +42% Q1 FY26 and backlog jumped to $14.5B (+44% YoY); Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition adds liquid-cooling exposure from chip to grid.

shifts.trend: EA backlog $13.2B FY25 (+31%) → $14.5B Q1 FY26 (+44% YoY, +32% organic); book-to-bill 1.2x both periods
shifts.note: Backlog accelerating — demand is outrunning deliveries, not stable

shifts.trend: EA revenue +16% FY25 → +20% Q1 FY26; EA organic orders +42% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Data center remains primary growth driver with machine OEM and residential adding in Q1

shifts.trend: EA operating margin 29.9% FY25 → 25.6% Q1 FY26 (-430bps); commodity and wage inflation the primary driver
shifts.note: Strong volume (+$840M) absorbing only part of the cost inflation — margin recovery will lag growth

shifts.trend: Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition (liquid cooling) announced FY25; closing Q2 2026; Fibrebond + Resilient acquired in FY25
shifts.note: Three consecutive liquid/thermal cooling acquisitions — deliberate platform build for AI data center cooling

ETN.verdict: Thesis intact. Backlog acceleration and Boyd Thermal acquisition confirm ETN is deliberately building the full power-and-cooling platform for data centers (chip to grid). Margin compression is the near-term risk; mgmt has guided it as commodity/wage timing, not structural.

FN.headline: DCI revenue +90.4% in FQ3 as inter-datacenter AI scale-across fabric drove the fastest-growing optical line; total revenue growth accelerated +35.9%→+39.3%.

shifts.trend: Revenue +35.9% FQ2 → +39.3% FQ3 YoY; 9-month revenue $3.33B +32.5%
shifts.note: Broad-based: all optical lines growing, DCI re-accelerating

shifts.trend: DCI $196.9M FQ3 (+90.4%) — fastest-growing line; Telecom $431.4M (+42.5%); HPC non-optical $106.7M
shifts.note: DCI is the AI scale-across proxy — inter-DC fabric for large-model training clusters

shifts.trend: 4 customers >10% in FQ3 (combined 59%) vs 2 customers (48.5%) FQ2; Nokia/Infinera + Lumentum consolidation flagged
shifts.note: Customer concentration broadened to 4 simultaneously — reflects multi-vendor optical ecosystem activity

shifts.trend: Gross margin 12.2% FQ2 → 11.9% FQ3 (-30bps); operating margin 10.1% → 9.9%; modest compression on content
shifts.note: Thin OSAT-like margins — scale helps but structural improvement requires ASP mix shift to higher-speed

FN.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. DCI +90% in FQ3 is the clearest signal that inter-DC AI fabric is scaling fast, and FN as the manufacturing partner for LITE/Infinera/Lumentum transceivers is directly in the path. The customer concentration spread to 4 simultaneously is a healthy demand diversification.

GEV.headline: RPO surged $150B→$163B in one quarter; HA-Turbine orders 43 FY25 vs 25 FY24 vs 8 FY23 — three-year demand inflection is structural; FCF $4.8B in Q1 alone.

shifts.trend: Power RPO $94.4B FY25 → $99.7B Q1 FY26 (+30% YoY); equipment RPO $64.2B→$75.9B in one quarter
shifts.note: Equipment backlog building faster than services — new turbine orders accelerating

shifts.trend: HA-Turbine orders 8→25→43 (FY23→FY24→FY25); Q1 FY26 HA orders 12 (vs 8 Q1 FY25); total GW orders 29.8 FY25 → 8.1 GW Q1 alone
shifts.note: Three-year demand inflection is compounding, not normalizing

shifts.trend: Power EBITDA margin 12.5%→14.7% FY25 → 16.3% Q1 FY26; favorable pricing + volume driving the step-up
shifts.note: Pricing power over backlog = margin expansion as older low-price contracts roll off

shifts.trend: FCF $3.7B FY25 → $4.8B Q1 FY26 alone (includes Prolec GE gain); Fitch upgraded BBB+, S&P BBB positive
shifts.note: Credit upgrade signals balance-sheet strengthening alongside growth

GEV.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating. HA-Turbine demand inflection is multi-year (8→25→43) and RPO growth confirms it has not peaked. Margin expansion in Power is the structural reward for pricing power in a supply-tight market. SMR optionality (Hitachi JV) adds long-duration upside.

GNRC.headline: C&I segment inflected from drag to +28% growth driver as data-center backup-power demand scales; residential remains weather-dependent but Enercon acquisition adds hyperscale custom power.

shifts.trend: C&I +4.9% FY25 → +28% Q1 FY26; data center customers cited as primary driver both periods
shifts.note: Large-megawatt diesel genset launch in FY25 is now generating revenue in Q1 FY26

shifts.trend: Total revenue -2% FY25 → +12% Q1 FY26; adjusted EBITDA $716M (17.0%) FY25 → $193M (+29%) Q1
shifts.note: Residential was the FY25 drag — C&I data-center is now the growth engine

shifts.trend: C&I margin 11.4% FY25 → 13.0% Q1 FY26 (+1.6pts); Residential 19.1%→25.1% on operational efficiencies
shifts.note: Both segments expanding margin simultaneously — positive operating leverage signal

shifts.trend: 'Large-megawatt backup market significantly supply-constrained' (FY25 10-K) — language maintained, Enercon acquisition adds hyperscale custom-power capability
shifts.note: Supply constraint on large-MW gensets is Generac's moat — they just entered this market

GNRC.verdict: Thesis building. GNRC entered the hyperscale-backup market just as demand went supply-constrained — timing was excellent. C&I inflection confirms data-center revenue is real. The risk is residential volatility (outage-season dependent) masking the C&I story; segment reporting reorganization in Q1 FY26 helps transparency.

GOOGL.headline: Cloud re-accelerated to +63% Q1 2026 vs +36% FY25; RPO nearly doubled to $467.6B in a single quarter after multi-GW TPU supply deals signed; capex $35.7B in Q1 alone.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud +36% FY25 → +63% Q1 2026 YoY; Cloud op income $13.9B FY25 → $6.6B Q1 2026 (47% of FY in one quarter)
shifts.note: Growth acceleration + margin expansion simultaneously — clearest AI-cloud momentum signal

shifts.trend: Cloud op margin 23.7% FY25 → 33% Q1 2026; Wiz ($29.5B) and Intersect ($5.9B) closed but not yet margin-dilutive
shifts.note: Three-fold YoY expansion in Cloud op income in Q1 is the headline number

shifts.trend: Capex $91.4B FY25 (vs $52.5B FY24) → $35.7B Q1 2026; FY26 to 'significantly increase' from $91.4B
shifts.note: Annual run-rate >$140B+ implied; servers, network equipment, data centers all increasing

shifts.trend: 'Multi-gigawatt' TPU hardware supply agreements signed Q1 2026 (revenue late 2026/majority 2027); RPO $242.8B→$467.6B
shifts.note: TPU supply deals are 3-7 year commitments — largest RPO jump in any company in the cohort

shifts.trend: $40B committed to unnamed private company (AI, $10B capital + $30B contingent); $16B into Waymo Q1
shifts.note: Capital deployment breadth increasing — less single-AI-partner dependency risk than MSFT/OpenAI

GOOGL.verdict: Thesis intact and arguably the strongest single-quarter print in the cohort. Cloud +63% with 33% op margin while capex accelerates to $35.7B in one quarter is the defining data point. TPU RPO jump to $467.6B suggests Google is winning multi-year enterprise AI infrastructure commitments.

HPE.headline: Juniper integration drove Networking from +51% to +151% as gross margin expanded 670bps; Cloud & AI server segment declining on GPU supply transitions and margin pressure from AI-server pass-through pricing.

shifts.trend: Networking +51.1% FY25 → +151.5% Q1 FY26 (full-quarter Juniper vs 4-month prior-year partial); AI Systems backlog growing
shifts.note: Juniper contribution finally shows full-quarter comparability — organic networking still strong

shifts.trend: GM ~28.8% FY25 → 35.9% Q1 FY26 (+670bps); non-GAAP op margin 9.8%→12.7% on Juniper software/service mix
shifts.note: Juniper's higher-margin networking software lifting the blended HPE margin profile

shifts.trend: Cloud & AI (Server+Storage) -2.7% Q1 FY26; AI servers growing but 'limited margins' explicitly cited; memory shortages flagged
shifts.note: AI server revenue is below the margin threshold where it adds to operating profit

shifts.trend: ARR $3.15B (+63%) FY25 on Networking; $600M Juniper synergy target by FY28 with $800M investment confirmed
shifts.note: ARR acceleration is the long-term software monetization thesis for Juniper

HPE.verdict: Partially intact. Juniper is delivering as a margin and growth catalyst for Networking. But the Cloud & AI segment's AI-server margin issue is a real structural concern — HPE is not capturing the AI compute wave economically, just passing through GPU cost. Thesis rests on Networking and software ARR compounding.

HUBB.headline: Electrical Solutions accelerated +7.1%→+11.8% on datacenter demand; Utility Solutions inflected to +10% Q1 on T&D strength; $2.16B backlog expected to ship largely in 2026.

shifts.trend: Electrical Solutions +7.1% FY25 → +11.8% Q1 FY26 organic; Utility Solutions +2.0%→+10% Q1
shifts.note: Both segments simultaneously accelerating for the first time — datacenter + grid infrastructure converging

shifts.trend: Adjusted op margin 22.5% FY25 (+80bps) → Q1 FY26 Electrical Solutions -30bps but Utility Solutions +190bps (net positive)
shifts.note: Commodity/tariff inflation offset by volume leverage — net margin expanding on consolidated basis

shifts.trend: Total backlog $2.16B YE25 (+14%) → substantially all to ship in 2026
shifts.note: Backlog provides near-term revenue visibility; not elongating further

shifts.trend: Datacenter and light industrial cited as primary Electrical growth driver both FY25 and Q1 FY26
shifts.note: No new disclosure on datacenter as % of revenue — qualitative signal consistent both quarters

HUBB.verdict: Thesis intact. HUBB is executing as the electrical-connectivity infrastructure play for data centers. Backlog visibility through 2026 and both segment accelerations simultaneously is the strongest signal. Tariff/commodity headwind is manageable given backlog-backed pricing.

INTC.headline: DCAI inflected to +22% Q1 2026 YoY but Intel Foundry external revenue was only $174M on a $17.5B+ cost base; supply constraints (substrates, memory) expected to persist through H2 2026.

shifts.trend: DCAI +$794M FY25 → $5.1B Q1 2026 (+22% YoY); CCG +1% Q1 on ASP+16% but volume-13%
shifts.note: DCAI is the one inflecting segment; server ASP +27% on premium mix + demand-based pricing

shifts.trend: 'Intel 7 supply constraints hit CCG/DCAI Q4 FY25 and expected to persist Q1 2026' (FY25 10-K) → 'Market demand exceeded available supply Q1 2026; expect through H2' (Q1 10-Q)
shifts.note: Supply constraint language unchanged and actually strengthened — bottleneck persisting

shifts.trend: Intel Foundry op loss $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 2026; external revenue $307M FY25 → $174M Q1 (Altera now external)
shifts.note: Foundry losses persisting with no external revenue acceleration visible

shifts.trend: $4.1B Q1 2026 restructuring drives $3.7B net loss; total restructuring $4.6B+ in 12 months; 159M-share US Gov warrant not yet monetizable
shifts.note: Capital allocation in restructuring mode — buybacks suspended, dividend cut

shifts.trend: SoftBank $2.0B + NVIDIA $5.0B equity investments; CHIPS Secure Enclave disbursements beginning; 18A at high-volume production
shifts.note: Strategic partnerships substituting for internal capital deficit

INTC.verdict: Inflection thesis only partially confirmed. DCAI +22% and 18A at high-volume production are genuine milestones. But Foundry external revenue remains tiny ($174M) and supply constraints are worsening rather than easing. The NVIDIA partnership is the pivotal signal — if it produces real Foundry revenue by Q3 2026, the thesis turns. Otherwise, it's restructuring in slow motion.

KLAC.headline: Revenue growth accelerated +7%→+11% YoY to a record quarter; Korea +80% on HBM memory investment; China fell from 30%→24% of revenue as export controls redirected spend.

shifts.trend: Revenue $3.30B (+7% YoY) Q2 → $3.42B (+11% YoY) Q3; services +18%→+16%; Korea $681M (+80%) Q3
shifts.note: Record quarter with broad-based growth across memory and foundry/logic

shifts.trend: China $995M (30.2%) Q2 → $830M (24.3%) Q3 — dollar declining, share falling 6pts in one quarter
shifts.note: Export-control effect accelerating: China revenue down $165M QoQ while Korea +$200M

shifts.trend: Gross margin 61.4% Q2 → 61.1% Q3 (-0.3pts); DRAM costs for image computers cited as transitory headwind
shifts.note: Extremely high-margin business barely moving — DRAM cost friction is small relative to base

shifts.trend: Q3 repurchases $626M + dividends $249M; 17th consecutive annual dividend increase to $2.30/share/qtr; $10.31B buyback remaining
shifts.note: Capital return acceleration coincides with record revenue — management confidence signal

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact. KLAC is benefiting from HBM-driven process control intensity (more inspection steps per node) and advanced 2nm foundry investment. China decline is accelerating but Korea and North America are absorbing the shift. Record quarter confirms the upgrade cycle is not over.

LEU.headline: Backlog grew $3.8B→$3.9B with $2.4B contingent on new capacity; $900M DOE HALEU task order and Fluor EPC contract mark the transition from R&D to construction; SWU spot price $200 (vs $34 historic low).

shifts.trend: SWU spot price $200 at Q1 FY26 end vs $34 historic low — +488% from trough; non-Russian market explicitly undersupplied
shifts.note: Price signal is the investment thesis — structural enrichment supply deficit driven by Russia sanctions

shifts.trend: $900M DOE HALEU task order Jan 2026; Fluor EPC contract Feb 2026; $560M+ Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing; $350-450M guided FY26 capex
shifts.note: Multiple capital commitments signed in Q1 — construction phase beginning

shifts.trend: Backlog $3.8B FY25 → $3.9B Q1 FY26 (+$100M); Technical Solutions +47% Q1 on HALEU Operation Contract
shifts.note: HALEU services growing faster than LEU on DOE contract work

shifts.trend: Russian TENEX supply (43% of global enrichment capacity) declining; domestic centrifuge production starting in Oak Ridge for supply diversification
shifts.note: Oklo JV exploration adds HALEU deconversion capability downstream — broadening the value chain

LEU.verdict: Thesis intact and transitioning from story to execution. Three Q1 FY26 catalysts (DOE task order, Fluor EPC, Oklo JV) are all de-risking milestones. Key risk: FY27 DOE budget doesn't fund HALEU cascade continuation — a government-dependent single-program risk at this stage.

LITE.headline: Gross margin structurally re-rated: 36.1%→44.2% (+1,540bps) in two quarters as 200G-lane supply allocation drives pricing power; supply-tightness language hardened from 'has led to' (FQ2) to 'required us' (FQ3).

shifts.trend: Gross margin 36.1% FQ2 → 44.2% FQ3 (+810bps QoQ, +1,540bps YoY); 9-month gross margin 38.8% (+1,300bps)
shifts.note: Fastest gross-margin re-rate in the optics cohort — structural, not one-time

shifts.trend: Revenue +65.5% FQ2 → +90.1% FQ3 YoY; 9-month revenue $2.01B +72.4%
shifts.note: Growth rate accelerating sequentially into a larger base — supply-allocation pricing multiplying revenue

shifts.trend: 'Demand outpacing supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation' (FQ2) → 'required us to make decisions on supply allocation' (FQ3)
shifts.note: Language shift from passive to active is a hard supply-constraint signal — LITE is rationing 1.6T InP/EML chips

shifts.trend: Top customer 24%→26% in FQ3; combined top-2 38% both quarters; OCS (optical circuit switches) >$10M FQ2 → >$25M FQ3
shifts.note: OCS is the new margin-accretive category; OCS customer concentration bears monitoring

LITE.verdict: Strongest bottleneck thesis in the optics layer. LITE is the InP/EML supply choke point for 1.6T transceivers and margin expansion confirms pricing power in a supply-allocated market. Two consecutive quarters of tightening language and accelerating margins with no easing signal = structurally tight through at least CY2027.

LRCX.headline: Memory mix jumped 34%→39% as DRAM/HBM investment surged; revenue grew +9% QoQ with China stable at ~34-35% and gross margin holding near 50% despite customer mix shifts.

shifts.trend: Memory mix 34% Q2 → 39% Q3 on strengthened DRAM/HBM; Foundry 59%→54% on weaker mature-node spend
shifts.note: DRAM/HBM investment cycle is the dominant driver — mirrors the MU and KLAC data

shifts.trend: Revenue $5.34B Q2 (flat QoQ) → $5.84B Q3 (+9% QoQ); nine-month revenue $16.5B vs $13.3B (+24% YoY)
shifts.note: Q2 was a consolidation quarter; Q3 resumption driven by memory capex

shifts.trend: China 35% Q2 → 34% Q3 of revenue; Korea jumped 20%→23%; Taiwan 23% both quarters
shifts.note: China share modestly declining but not collapsing — non-memory China demand providing floor

shifts.trend: Gross margin 49.6% Q2 → 49.8% Q3 (+0.2pts) on better factory efficiencies
shifts.note: GM holding near 50% despite customer mix shift is a positive cost-structure signal

shifts.trend: Q3 capital returns: $1.16B repurchases + $326M dividends + $751M debt paydown; cash declined to $4.77B
shifts.note: Aggressive capital return during strong cycle — management signaling cycle durability

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact. DRAM/HBM mix driving Q3 acceleration confirms LRCX participates in both the etch-for-logic and etch-for-memory cycles simultaneously. China stability at ~34% removes the tail risk of a sudden step-down. Gross margin holding near 50% is a quality-of-earnings signal.

META.headline: Capex guidance raised twice in 6 months: $72.2B FY25 → $115-135B guide → $125-145B raised Q1 26; revenue growth re-accelerated to +33% Q1 as ad impression/price both expanded.

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $72.2B → FY26 guide $115-135B (Feb 2026) → raised to $125-145B (Apr 2026 Q1 10-Q); Q1 capex $19.8B
shifts.note: Second consecutive guidance raise in one year — AI build conviction is deepening, not moderating

shifts.trend: FY25 +22% → Q1 2026 +33% YoY; ad impressions +12%→+19%, price per ad +9%→+12%
shifts.note: Core ad engine re-accelerating alongside AI infrastructure build — both sides of the flywheel positive

shifts.trend: Non-cancelable contractual commitments $237.7B at Mar 31 2026 (cloud, servers, DCs); $182.9B off-balance-sheet leases not yet commenced
shifts.note: Total committed infrastructure exposure >$420B — enormous but matches the revenue scale

shifts.trend: Reality Labs op loss $19.2B FY25 → $4.03B Q1 alone; similar FY26 loss guided; AI investments increasing R&D
shifts.note: RL losses structurally elevated but well-disclosed — not worsening on a full-year basis

META.verdict: Thesis intact. Capex raised twice in 6 months and revenue acceleration in Q1 confirms the AI investment is driving growth, not just costs. The $125-145B capex guide with $33% revenue growth puts META in the rare category of infrastructure-lead compounders. RL losses are the only structural drag — contained and guided.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions data-center revenue grew $67M Q2 → $130M Q3 (+93%); Modine announced Performance Tech spin-off (Gentherm RMT) to become a pure-play data-center cooling company.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions +24% Q2 → +51% Q3 YoY; data center contribution +$67M→+$130M QoQ sequential jump
shifts.note: Data center cooling revenue doubling sequentially every two quarters

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions margin 13.7% Q2 → 15.3% Q3 (recovering from ramp inefficiencies); still below pre-ramp 17%+ level
shifts.note: Manufacturing efficiency improving as US capacity build matures

shifts.trend: 9-month Climate Solutions data-center product sales +$221.7M YoY; hyperscale/colocation NA and Europe cited consistently
shifts.note: Geographic consistency suggests this is multi-hyperscaler demand, not single-customer

shifts.trend: $100M incremental capex for US data-center manufacturing announced Q2; Q3 incremental lease signed; Performance Tech spin-off unlocks focus
shifts.note: Gentherm RMT frees ~$1B enterprise value; MOD retains the fastest-growing segment

MOD.verdict: Thesis intact and sharpening. The Gentherm spin-off is the key signal — management is betting the entire company on data-center cooling. Two-quarter data-center revenue trajectory ($67M→$130M of YoY growth) confirms the bet is paying off. Pure-play valuation re-rate is the next catalyst if margin recovers toward 17%.

MRVL.headline: Data center end-market grew from $1.52B (73% mix) in Q3 to $6.1B (74%, +46%) full-year FY26; Celestial AI + XConn acquisitions locked in photonic fabric and CXL/PCIe switching roadmap.

shifts.trend: DC end-market Q3 FY26: $1.52B (+38% YoY) → FY26 full-year $6.1B (+46% YoY); total FY26 +42%
shifts.note: DC share of revenue holding at 73-74% while absolute DC growth rate is accelerating

shifts.trend: Q3 GM 51.6% (distorted by prior-year impairments) → FY26 GM 51.0%; underlying operating leverage on AI-ASIC mix
shifts.note: Gross margin at 51% is a recovery story — roadmap to 60%+ as custom ASICs scale

shifts.trend: Distributor A 38% Q3 → 37% FY26 (hyperscaler conduit); direct Customer A 11%→14%; Communications end-market $2.1B (+31%)
shifts.note: Distributor concentration flat but direct customer share rising — healthier mix

shifts.trend: Celestial AI ~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares (photonic fabric); XConn $280M + 2.1M shares (CXL/PCIe); automotive ethernet sold $2.5B (Infineon)
shifts.note: Net M&A spends offset by automotive divestiture — strategic pivot to AI interconnect complete

shifts.trend: Communications end-market ($2.1B, +31% FY26) recovering from inventory correction; consolidated into single segment from Q4 FY26
shifts.note: Non-DC simplification signals confidence that DC will dominate disclosure going forward

MRVL.verdict: Thesis intact. MRVL is executing the custom AI ASIC + interconnect platform thesis (SerDes + ARM compute + silicon photonics + CPO + custom HBM). Celestial AI and XConn fill the two missing pieces. The next test is whether Distributor A concentration at 37% is sustainable or a hyperscaler consolidation risk.

MSFT.headline: Azure re-accelerated from +39% to +40% and commercial RPO doubled to $625-627B; capex sustained $29.9B→$30.9B per quarter as AI infrastructure build shows no sign of moderation.

shifts.trend: Azure +39% Q2 → +40% Q3 YoY; MS Cloud +26%→+29%; total revenue +17%→+18%
shifts.note: Acceleration (even marginal) into an already-large base is the story

shifts.trend: Commercial RPO $625B (+110% Q2) → $627B (+99% Q3); Microsoft now has near-equivalent RPO to the entire US federal discretionary budget
shifts.note: RPO growth rate normalizing on a massive base; absolute scale is the moat

shifts.trend: Capex $29.9B Q2 → $30.9B Q3; 9-month $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year — exactly 2x YoY pace
shifts.note: Capex doubling YoY and holding steady sequentially — maximum sustainable infrastructure build

shifts.trend: MS Cloud gross margin 67% (both quarters) — pressured by AI infra but holding; operating income +21%→+20%
shifts.note: Cloud GM holding at 67% despite the capex doubling is a positive discipline signal

shifts.trend: $5.9B non-operating gain from OpenAI Recapitalization dilution gain (OpenAI → public benefit corp) recorded in 9M
shifts.note: OpenAI recapitalization creates a one-time accounting gain — not recurring

MSFT.verdict: Thesis intact. Azure re-acceleration to +40% with $627B commercial RPO and 9-month capex of $80.1B is definitionally the largest AI infrastructure buildout in the world. Cloud GM holding at 67% proves the infra costs are being absorbed. OpenAI dependency remains the key counterparty risk.

MXL.headline: Infrastructure segment recovered strongly (+$34.3M FY25 → +$36.3M Q1 alone) on optical DSP and wireless backhaul; customer concentration broadened as top customer fell from >20% to 13%.

shifts.trend: FY25 +30% YoY → Q1 2026 +43% YoY; Infrastructure +$34.3M FY25 → +$36.3M in Q1 alone on optical and backhaul
shifts.note: One-quarter infrastructure contribution nearly equals FY25 full-year — optical DSP cycle accelerating

shifts.trend: Top customer historically >20% → 13% Q1 2026; top 10 customers 65% FY25 → 56% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Significant customer base broadening — reduces single-customer concentration risk substantially

shifts.trend: Gross margin 57% FY25 → maintained Q1 2026 on volume-led growth with no material price changes
shifts.note: Stable GM at 57% on volume growth is positive operating leverage signal

shifts.trend: Infrastructure growing fastest on optical DSPs for 400G→800G→1.6T switch transition explicitly described as roadmap
shifts.note: 1.6T cycle is MRVL's networking market but MXL's optical DSP chips go inside the transceiver

MXL.verdict: Thesis building. MXL is positioned on the optical DSP inside 1.6T transceivers — the same cycle as LITE/COHR/FN but upstream in the signal-processing layer. The customer diversification improvement reduces prior concentration risk. Key risk: whether MXL's optical DSP wins at 1.6T or MRVL/Credo take share.

NOW.headline: Revenue and RPO growth holding steady at 21-22% and 25% respectively; Now Assist AI agents are embedded across the platform but consumption-model upside is not yet visible in RPO metrics.

shifts.trend: Revenue +21% FY25 → +22% Q1 2026; subscription +21%→+22%; growth rate consistent but not accelerating
shifts.note: Unlike hyperscalers, NOW is not showing AI-driven re-acceleration yet

shifts.trend: RPO $28.2B (+27%) FY25 → $27.7B Q1 2026 (+25%); cRPO $13.0B (+25%)→+23%; backlog slightly decelerating
shifts.note: cRPO growth decelerating modestly — no step-change from AI agent upsell in the metrics

shifts.trend: Self-hosted federal revenue $492M FY25 → Q1 $138M (vs $157M prior Q1, declining); subscriptions driving the mix
shifts.note: Federal on-prem declining as cloud subscription migrates

shifts.trend: Subscription gross margin steady; FY26 effective tax rate expected to benefit from immediate domestic R&D expensing (OBBBA)
shifts.note: Tax tailwind from OBBBA not yet quantified but directionally positive

NOW.verdict: Story intact at current growth rates but needs an AI-agent acceleration catalyst to justify premium valuation. RPO growth is consistent but not inflecting upward — the Now Assist consumption model needs to show up as a cRPO uplift at a future print. Steady compounder, not a re-acceleration story yet.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection organic growth jumped from ~17% FY25 to +50.1% Q1 FY26 with margin expanding 220bps; combined order backlog more than doubled to $2.3B and capex tripling to $425-525M in FY26.

shifts.trend: SP organic +~17% FY25 → +50.1% Q1 FY26; total revenue +29.5%→+53.5% YoY
shifts.note: Infrastructure/data-center contribution jumped from ~16pts to ~46pts of SP organic growth in one quarter

shifts.trend: SP segment margin 20.7% FY25 → 22.7% Q1 FY26 (+200bps); consolidated GM compressed 290bps on tariffs/capacity
shifts.note: Segment-level margin expanding despite blended compression — data-center product mix is structurally higher margin

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guided $425-525M (2x step-up); EPG acquisition $1.0B (May 2025) adding enclosures/liquid cooling
shifts.note: Capex doubling signals multi-year capacity commitment to data-center demand

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $2.3B at YE25 (doubled YoY); majority firm expected to ship in next 12-18 months
shifts.note: Backlog depth provides near-term revenue certainty through end of FY26

NVT.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating. NVT is executing as the enclosures-and-liquid-cooling platform for AI data centers. The SP organic jump from ~17% to +50% in one quarter with margin expansion is the most bullish datapoint. EPG acquisition adds further capacity and product breadth. Tariff/raw-material inflation is the key margin risk.

OKLO.headline: Order book grew to ~18,100 MWe (+2,500% since SPAC); Meta signed a $1.2 GW prepayment agreement Jan 2026 — first hyperscaler prepayment for advanced fission power; FY26 cash burn $80-100M guided.

shifts.trend: Order book ~15,600 MWe Q3 FY25 → ~18,100 MWe (adding 4,750 MWe data-center LOIs in Q3); Meta 1.2 GW prepayment Jan 2026
shifts.note: Meta prepayment is the most significant de-risking event — converts LOI to committed capital

shifts.trend: 9M FY25 investing $605.9M → FY26 capex guided $350-450M; Pike County Ohio land acquired; Tennessee fuel center roadmap $1.68B
shifts.note: Capital commitments escalating significantly — execution risk rising with scale

shifts.trend: NRC Phase I pre-application assessment (no gaps) Q3 FY25 → PDC topical report accepted on accelerated timeline → Aurora Design Agreement approved Feb 2026
shifts.note: Three consecutive NRC milestones in six months — regulatory de-risking fastest in advanced-fission cohort

shifts.trend: 12 GW Switch master agreement + Equinix/Diamondback/Prometheus LOIs → Meta 1.2 GW prepayment adds committed capacity
shifts.note: Transition from non-binding to binding commitments accelerating

OKLO.verdict: Pre-revenue but milestone-rich. The Meta prepayment is the pivotal signal — it's the first binding hyperscaler commitment to advanced fission power. The NRC trajectory is the fastest in the sector. Key risk: first Aurora deployment in 2028 is 2+ years away; any schedule slip resets the thesis materially.

ORCL.headline: OCI revenue growth accelerated +68%→+84% YoY; RPO climbed $523.3B→$552.6B; 9-month capex $39.2B (3x prior year) funded by $47.7B in new senior notes + mandatory convert.

shifts.trend: OCI +68% Q2 FY26 → +84% Q3 FY26 YoY; total revenue +14%→+22%; cloud apps +11%→+13%
shifts.note: OCI growth rate is the fastest-accelerating infrastructure cloud after GOOGL — 80%+ territory

shifts.trend: H1 capex $20.5B → 9M $39.2B (3x prior year 9M $12.1B); issued $42.7B senior notes + $5B mandatory convertible preferred
shifts.note: Financing at scale to fund the OCI build — $47.7B in debt in one year

shifts.trend: RPO $523.3B Q2 → $552.6B Q3 (vs $97.3B and $130.2B YoY); 5x+ growth in committed cloud revenue
shifts.note: RPO trajectory suggests Oracle has signed enough cloud contracts to sustain multi-year OCI growth

shifts.trend: 9M operating margin declining as infrastructure spend front-loads; cloud+software expenses +49%; margin % compressed
shifts.note: Near-term margin compression is deliberate — Oracle is choosing growth over current profitability

ORCL.verdict: Thesis intact. OCI +84% is the clearest signal that enterprises are diversifying AI workloads beyond AWS/Azure/GCP. The RPO at $552.6B provides extraordinary revenue visibility. Key risk: $47.7B of new debt in one year — if OCI growth slows before the cash flow inflects, leverage becomes the story.

PLTR.headline: Commercial revenue growth re-accelerated to +95% Q1 2026 vs +60% FY25; operating margin jumped from ~31% to 46% in one quarter; no customer >10% of Q1 revenue — diversification achieved.

shifts.trend: Commercial revenue +60% FY25 → +95% Q1 2026; Government +53% FY25 → +76% Q1; total +53% FY25 → +85% Q1
shifts.note: Both segments accelerating simultaneously — broad-based demand inflection

shifts.trend: Op margin ~31% FY25 → 46% Q1 2026; op income $1.41B FY25 → $754M in Q1 alone (+328% YoY)
shifts.note: Operating leverage is extraordinary — revenue doubling with margin expanding 15pts

shifts.trend: Founders voting 22% FY25 → No customer >10% revenue Q1 2026; US revenue 74%→79%
shifts.note: Customer concentration risk resolved — broad-based commercial AIP adoption

shifts.trend: Government contribution margin 62%→73%; Commercial 61%→73%; both segments simultaneously expanding
shifts.note: Margin convergence across segments signals scaling unit economics on AIP platform

PLTR.verdict: Thesis intact and strongest print in the software layer. Commercial +95% with 46% op margin in one quarter resolves the prior debate about whether PLTR could commercialize AIP at scale. No >10% customer concentration means the growth is broad, not lumpy. The question now is sustainability of the +85% growth rate — any deceleration will be heavily penalized at current multiples.

PWR.headline: Backlog grew $43.98B→$48.47B (+10% sequentially in one quarter) and RPO hit $26.24B; Q1 operating income +42% on data-center-driven electric and underground infrastructure.

shifts.note: Backlog building sequentially every quarter — demand is outrunning execution capacity

shifts.trend: Revenue +20.3% FY25 → +26.3% Q1 FY26; operating income +20.5% FY25 → +41.7% Q1
shifts.note: Operating leverage inflecting sharply in Q1 — execution efficiency improving on high volumes

shifts.trend: Gross margin 13.4% FY25 → 14.1% Q1 FY26 (+0.7pts); op margin 3.8%→4.3%
shifts.note: Modest but consistent margin expansion as high-complexity data-center work gains share of mix

shifts.trend: Underground +50.3% FY25 on DSI acquisition (hyperscaler mechanical/plumbing); Electric +21% FY25 → +$1.52B Q1
shifts.note: Both segments accelerating — data-center engagement broadening from electrical to inside the facility

PWR.verdict: Thesis intact. PWR is the pure-play data-center construction enabler and backlog depth ($48.47B, 10% QoQ build) is the most defensible forward-revenue signal in the portfolio. Operating leverage is inflecting. The risk is execution capacity — backlog growth is outpacing the workforce and supply chain.

SNOW.headline: Revenue growth held at 29-31% across the two periods; NRR stable at 125%; $1M+ customers grew 27%; RPO recovered from $7.9B to $9.8B — steady-state compounder with AI consumption upside still ahead.

shifts.trend: Product revenue +29% Q3 FY26 → FY26 +31%; total customers +21%; $1M+ customers 576→733 (+27%)
shifts.note: Growth rate consistent and broad-based; no AI-driven re-acceleration visible yet

shifts.trend: Forbes 2000 customers ~43% of revenue; 790 customers vs prior; RPO weighted-avg life 2.7 years
shifts.note: Customer base broadening across global enterprises — diversified demand base

shifts.trend: RPO $7.9B (Oct) → $9.8B (Jan); 46-48% to recognize next 12 months; NRR 125% steady
shifts.note: NRR modestly off 126% peak but holding — consumption expansion is occurring

shifts.trend: SBC declining as % of revenue from 41%→34%; OCF $1.2B FY26; net loss $1.3B but cash-generative on adjusted basis
shifts.note: Improving cash efficiency on a constant-currency basis — unit economics improving

SNOW.verdict: Steady compounder thesis intact. SNOW is not showing AI re-acceleration in the metrics yet, but 125% NRR and $1M+ customer growth suggest consumption is expanding. The catalysts to watch are Cortex AI/ML workload adoption appearing in NRR and any RPO build acceleration from AI workload commitments.

SNPS.headline: Ansys integration on track — revenue +66% Q1 FY26 with $885.6M Ansys contribution; Design IP declining -6% but backlog steady at $11.3B; $3.5B term loan paid down Q1 ahead of buyback resumption.

shifts.trend: Revenue +15% FY25 (+$756.6M Ansys) → +66% Q1 FY26 (+$885.6M Ansys); organic growth ex-Ansys positive in most geographies
shifts.note: Headline +66% is primarily Ansys consolidation — organic growth moderating post-acquisition

shifts.trend: Design IP -6% Q1 FY26 (muted expected FY26); Design Automation +96% on Ansys volume
shifts.note: Design IP weakness from BIS effects, foundry softness, and internal roadmap issues — confirmed at Q1

shifts.trend: China -22% ex-Ansys FY25 → 'challenging near-term' repeated Q1 FY26; broader Trade Restrictions impacts
shifts.note: China EDA market remains constrained two consecutive periods with no improvement signal

shifts.trend: $13.5B debt at FY25-end → $3.5B term loan paid off Q1 FY26; buyback suspended pending further paydown
shifts.note: Aggressive deleveraging — management prioritizing balance sheet before shareholder returns

shifts.trend: Backlog $11.4B FY25 → $11.3B Q1 FY26 (stable); 45%→47% to recognize next 12 months
shifts.note: Near-term revenue recognition slightly higher — demand composition improving

SNPS.verdict: Integration thesis intact but with a Design IP overhang. Ansys is contributing as modeled and deleveraging is ahead of schedule. Design IP weakness (-6% Q1) is the one item that needs to stabilize before the full Ansys+SNPS thesis is confirmed. China EDA restrictions remain unresolved — tail risk.

VRT.headline: Americas revenue growth stepped up +41.9%→+53.1% with margin expanding 220bps to 27.0%; gross margin +400bps to 37.7% Q1; backlog doubled and capex tripling to $425-525M to meet demand.

shifts.trend: Americas +41.9% FY25 → +53.1% Q1 FY26; total revenue +27.7%→+30.1%; EMEA -20.3% Q1 partially offsetting
shifts.note: Americas (62% of mix) is the AI data-center demand engine — EMEA timing drag is temporary

shifts.trend: Gross margin 36.3% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 FY26 (+140bps annually, +400bps QoQ spike); Americas op margin 26.8%→27.0%
shifts.note: GM expansion driven by data-center product mix enrichment and operating leverage — two consecutive periods

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $15.0B at YE25 (2x from $7.2B YE24); majority firm, expected to ship next 12-18 months
shifts.note: Backlog visibility is extraordinarily strong — 12-18 month forward revenue is largely committed

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guided $425-525M (2x step-up); Q1 capex $114M pacing toward midpoint
shifts.note: Capacity expansion underway; $425-525M annualized is the new normal for VRT

VRT.verdict: Strongest thesis in the power/cooling layer. Americas acceleration, gross-margin expansion, and doubled backlog simultaneously is the best combination in the cohort. The $425-525M capex guides capacity for the next 2+ years of demand. EMEA weakness is timing (delivery cadence), not demand — watch the next print.
